title: A constitution survives defeat
content: After Sweden's military pressure in 1814, Norway entered union but kept its constitution and parliament. This compromise preserved crucial self-governing institutions. Norway lost full independence but kept a legal platform for future sovereignty.
next: How did this framework influence 19th-century nation-building?

title: Nation-building inside a union
content: Norway modernized economically through shipping, timber, fisheries, and infrastructure. Education expanded, and urban middle classes grew. Internal development made the country increasingly capable of independent statehood.
next: Why did language and culture become political?

title: Cultural nationalism and language politics
content: Writers, artists, and linguists cultivated distinct Norwegian identity after long Danish dominance. Debates over written language forms reflected deeper questions about who represented the nation. Culture became a tool of political legitimacy.
next: What issue finally broke the union with Sweden?

title: Constitutional struggle with Sweden
content: The conflict over separate consular services exposed Norway's demand for equal sovereignty. Parliamentary tactics and political mobilization intensified pressure. Independence was pursued through institutions more than armed rebellion.
next: How did 1905 avoid becoming a major war?

title: Independence through referendum
content: A near-unanimous referendum backed dissolution of the union, and negotiations prevented full-scale conflict. Norway then chose a constitutional monarchy under Haakon VII. The state entered the 20th century with strong democratic legitimacy.
next: How did neutrality fare in the world wars?

title: Neutrality tested, occupation endured
content: World War I neutrality brought profits and losses in shipping, while World War II brought German occupation and collaborationist rule. Resistance movements, exile government, and maritime contribution became central to national memory. Occupation permanently changed security thinking.

title: Social democracy and welfare expansion
content: Postwar governments built broad welfare institutions: health, education, labor protections, and redistribution. Norway joined NATO but balanced alliance with cautious base policy. A high-trust, egalitarian political model took shape.
next: What transformed Norway's economic trajectory in 1969?

title: Oil discovered, rules established
content: North Sea oil and gas could have created boom-bust instability. Instead, Norway used high taxation, state participation, and domestic capability-building to retain public control. Resource policy became institution-building, not just extraction.
next: How was oil wealth managed for future generations?

title: The sovereign wealth strategy
content: The Government Pension Fund Global invested resource revenues abroad to reduce domestic overheating and save for the future. This separated short-term politics from long-term wealth management. It became a global model for intergenerational fiscal prudence.
next: What tensions define Norway in the 21st century?

title: Prosperity with new dilemmas
content: Modern Norway ranks high in welfare, trust, and equality, yet faces integration debates, security concerns, and climate contradictions linked to fossil exports. The core challenge is balancing oil-era wealth with post-carbon responsibility. Norway's story remains open, not finished.
next: Test your understanding of modern Norway.
